sections.title: Connect drag to force balance next

paragraphs: This page follows one object released from rest through one fluid. The downward weight stays fixed at $mg$, while the upward drag force starts near zero and grows with speed according to $F_d = kAv^2$. That changing force balance is why the fall does not behave like constant-acceleration free fall.

Terminal velocity is the speed where drag has grown enough to balance the weight. The object does not stop there. It keeps moving downward, but once drag and weight are nearly equal, the net force is near zero, so the speed stops increasing much.

Each control changes a different part of that balance. More mass increases the weight, so a higher speed is needed before drag can match it. More area or a larger drag-strength constant makes drag stronger at the same speed, so the object reaches balance sooner and at a lower terminal speed.

keyIdeas: Drag starts small because the object starts with little speed, then grows rapidly as the fall speeds up.
Weight stays constant while drag changes, so the net force is largest early and much smaller near terminal speed.
Terminal velocity means drag and weight are balancing, so acceleration becomes nearly zero.
At terminal velocity the object is still moving downward; what fades is the change in speed, not the motion itself.
A larger mass raises terminal speed, while a larger area or stronger drag lowers it.

commonMisconception.myth: Terminal velocity happens because gravity turns off after the object has been falling for a while.
correction: Gravity is still acting; the weight force stays essentially constant in this model.
The changing part is drag. It grows with speed until it nearly matches weight, leaving very little net downward force.

workedExamples.title: Drag worked examples
workedExamples.intro: Use the live bench values. First predict the terminal speed from the current controls, then use the current time and speed to read the live force balance.

items.title: Predict the current terminal speed
items.prompt: For the current setup with $m = {{massValue}}$, $A = {{areaValue}}$, and $k = {{dragStrengthValue}}$, what terminal speed does this drag model predict?

steps.label: 1. Turn the mass into the constant weight
2. Use the force-balance condition for terminal speed
3. Solve for the speed where drag matches weight

applyAction.label: Load Heavy sphere
applyAction.observationHint: The weight arrow is larger, so the speed curve levels off at a higher value before drag can match it.

items.title: Read the force balance right now
items.prompt: At $t = {{timeValue}}$, the object is moving at ${{speedValue}}\,\mathrm{m/s}$. What drag force and net downward force does that give right now?

steps.label: 1. Read the current speed from the stage or speed graph
2. Use the drag law at that speed
3. Compare drag with weight to find the net force

applyAction.label: Load Draggy disk
applyAction.observationHint: The speed curve flattens quickly, the drag arrow catches the weight, and the net-force line drops close to zero.

miniChallenge.prompt: Two objects have the same drag strength and the same frontal area, but one has twice the mass. Which one has the higher terminal speed?
miniChallenge.prediction: Use the force balance $kAv_t^2 = mg$: which setup needs drag to grow to a larger value before it can balance weight?
miniChallenge.explanation: The heavier object has the larger constant weight. With the same $k$ and area, drag can match that larger weight only at a higher speed, so the terminal speed is higher.

equations.meaning: In this bounded model, drag gets much larger as speed increases.
Terminal speed is the speed where upward drag has grown enough to match the downward weight.
A larger mass raises terminal speed, while a larger area or stronger drag lowers it.

variableLinks.description: Raises the constant weight. With the same area and drag strength, the object must reach a higher speed before drag can balance it.
Sets how much drag the object gets at the same speed. A larger area lowers the speed needed for drag to catch the weight.
Packages how strongly this fluid-and-shape model resists motion. A larger $k$ makes drag stronger at every speed and lowers terminal speed.

controls.description: Changes the object's mass and therefore the constant weight that drag must eventually balance.
Changes how much drag the object gets at the same speed.
Sets how strongly this bounded model resists motion through the fluid.

presets.description: A larger area makes drag catch up sooner, so terminal speed is lower.
Stronger drag lowers the eventual fall speed without changing the mass.
Doubling both mass and area keeps the same terminal-speed ratio when drag strength stays fixed.
Large area and strong drag produce a low terminal speed quickly.

whatToNotice: At first the weight arrow is much larger than the drag arrow, but the drag arrow grows with speed until the two nearly match.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes terminal velocity a force-balance idea instead of a memorized speed limit.

whatToNotice: Near terminal speed the object is still moving downward. What becomes small is the net force and acceleration.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates constant speed from zero motion.

whatToNotice: After the speed curve flattens, the object still keeps covering more distance.
overlays.whyItMatters: It prevents the mistake of confusing constant speed with stopping.

graphs.description: The speed rises quickly at first and then bends toward the flat terminal-speed line as drag catches up to weight.
Weight stays flat, drag rises with speed, and the net downward force falls toward zero.
Heavier objects need a higher speed before drag can balance the larger weight.
More area means more drag at the same speed, so terminal speed falls.
A larger drag-strength constant lowers the speed needed for force balance.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep one force question in view at a time so the stage, time rail, and graphs all tell the same fall story.

items.text: Right after release, drag is still small because the speed is still small. The weight arrow is already there, so the net downward force starts large.
Near terminal speed, the object is still moving downward even though the net force is tiny. Force balance makes the acceleration nearly vanish; it does not erase the speed that is already there.
items.whyItMatters: This is the key difference between terminal velocity and coming to rest.

items.text: On the mass sweep, larger mass raises terminal speed because the weight is larger while the drag law itself is unchanged.
On the area sweep, a larger frontal area lowers terminal speed because the same speed now creates a larger drag force.
Use compare mode to match the same terminal speed with two different setups. Equal terminal speed does not mean mass, area, and drag strength are all the same. It means the ratio $mg/(kA)$ matches.
items.tryThis: Start from Baseline drop, copy it into compare mode, then turn Setup B into Matched ratio. The terminal-speed labels match even though Setup B is both heavier and larger.

predictionMode.intro: Predict which side of the force balance changes before you run the new setup.

items.explanation: Area appears directly in the drag law $F_d = kAv^2$, so a larger area makes drag stronger at every speed and lowers terminal speed.
items.observationHint: The drag line catches the weight sooner, and the terminal-speed-area graph drops as area grows.

items.explanation: Mass changes the weight side of the balance, so a heavier object reaches a higher terminal speed even though the drag law itself is unchanged.
items.observationHint: The speed-history curve approaches a higher flat terminal-speed value when you switch to the heavier setup.

items.explanation: A larger $k$ makes drag stronger for the same $A$ and $v$, so the balance with weight happens at a lower speed.
items.observationHint: The terminal-speed-drag-strength graph slopes downward, and the force-balance graph reaches near-equality earlier.

challengeMode.intro: Use the falling-body bench to prove two terminal-velocity claims: tune a slower fall by changing the drag side of the balance, then pause at the moment drag and weight nearly match.

items.title: Build a slower terminal-speed case
items.prompt: Starting from Baseline drop, keep the mass near $2\,\mathrm{kg}$ and lower the terminal speed by increasing both area and drag strength.
items.successMessage: You lowered the terminal speed by strengthening the drag side of the balance. The object still falls, but it settles into a slower steady-speed regime.

setup.note: Use the terminal-speed graphs and keep the mass close to baseline.

hints: Keep mass near baseline, so the speed change must come from the drag side of the balance.
A larger area and a stronger drag constant both lower terminal speed; combine them instead of moving only one control.

targets.label: Raise the drag strength to about $18$.
Bring the terminal speed into the $3.2$ to $3.5\,\mathrm{m/s}$ range.

items.title: Catch the near-terminal moment
items.prompt: Starting from Draggy disk, pause when drag is almost equal to weight and the remaining net downward force is tiny.
items.successMessage: You found the near-terminal moment. The object is still moving, but the force imbalance is now so small that the speed is hardly changing.

setup.note: Pause the fall and scrub until the drag and weight readouts are almost matched.

hints: Look for the drag line almost touching the weight line while the net-force line sits close to zero.
Late in this low-terminal-speed setup, the speed is still positive even though the force imbalance is tiny.

targets.label: Make the net downward force smaller than about $0.35\,\mathrm{N}$.
Bring the current speed within about $0.10\,\mathrm{m/s}$ of terminal speed.

quickTest.intro: Use the force story, not just the formula. These checks ask whether you can read the graphs and explain the balance.

questions.explanation: Area sits directly in the drag law, so a larger area means the same speed produces more drag and the balance with weight happens earlier.

showMeAction.label: Load Wide canopy
showMeAction.observationHint: The terminal-speed-area graph slopes downward, and the speed curve flattens at a smaller value.

questions.explanation: Mass changes the weight side of the balance. The drag law is unchanged, so the speed at balance must rise.

showMeAction.label: Load Heavy sphere
showMeAction.observationHint: The weight arrow is larger, and the terminal-speed-mass graph rises as mass increases.

questions.explanation: Terminal velocity is a force-balance statement, not a stopped-motion statement. The speed is steady because the net force is near zero.

showMeAction.label: Load Draggy disk
showMeAction.observationHint: Late in the fall the speed is still positive even though the force lines are almost balanced.

questions.explanation: The drag law on this page depends on speed. With almost no speed yet, the drag force is still tiny even though the object is already in the fluid.

showMeAction.label: Restart Baseline drop
showMeAction.observationHint: Near $t = 0$, the drag line begins near zero while the weight line is already flat and positive.

questions.explanation: Terminal speed depends on the ratio $mg/(kA)$. Doubling both mass and area keeps that ratio the same when $k$ is fixed.

showMeAction.label: Load Matched ratio
showMeAction.observationHint: The control values are different, but the terminal-speed label matches the baseline because mass and area scaled together.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one object falling through one fluid column with a distance ruler on the left and a readout card on the right. The object width changes with area, while mass is represented through the force readouts rather than by resizing the object. Optional overlays show a constant downward weight arrow, an upward drag arrow that grows with speed, a net-force arrow, a terminal-speed cue, and a distance guide.
The time rail controls one bounded fall from rest. Compare mode can ghost a second setup behind the current one so two force-balance stories stay on the same scale.
The readout card reports mass, area, drag strength, distance fallen, current speed, terminal speed, drag force, and net downward force.
The speed-history graph is the main motion graph. The speed rises quickly and then bends toward the constant terminal-speed line.
The force-balance graph keeps the forces explicit: weight stays flat, drag rises, and the net downward force falls toward zero.
The response graphs isolate mass, area, and drag strength. The mass sweep rises, while the area and drag-strength sweeps fall because stronger drag-side factors lower terminal speed.

pageIntro.definition: Drop one object through a fluid, then change its mass, area, and drag strength to see why the fall settles into a steady terminal speed.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: Terminal velocity happens when upward drag has grown enough to balance weight, so acceleration fades while the object keeps moving downward.
